Maheegan Review - Empires & Puzzles 4-Star Hero Strategy Guide

Is Maheegan just a lone wolf or the leader of your 4-star pack? We track down the truth about this Plains Hunter, analyzing his fiendish skills, his aging stats, and why he might just be the secret MVP of your next Bloody Battle tournament. Read our full breakdown to see if he deserves the meat from your feeder heroes.
Maheegan: The Fiend-Summoning Wolf of the Plains
Maheegan is a 4-star Fire (Red) hero from the

family, originally released during the Covenant Summon event in late 2022. Designed as a hybrid damage dealer and fiend summoner, he brings a mix of direct impact and attrition to the battlefield. While he doesn't have a costume to freshen up his wardrobe (or stats), his base kit offers some interesting mechanics that can still find a home in specific niches. He’s the kind of guy who brings his own "pets" to the party—pets that eat your healing.

Maheegan sports a rugged, "I live in the woods and don't own a razor" aesthetic. Clad in leather armor with fur accents (presumably from something he hunted, or perhaps just a very aggressive thrift store find), he wields Wolverine-style claw weapons. The red war paint and intense stare scream "Plains Hunter," fitting perfectly with his family theme. It’s a solid, thematic design that clearly communicates his role as a relentless tracker and fighter.
Let's be blunt: Maheegan's stats are showing their age. With a base power of

, he sits significantly below the modern 4-star benchmark. Current top-tier 4-stars (like those from the Construct or Astral families) often boast Power levels approaching 800, with attack and defense stats frequently exceeding 800. Maheegan is essentially a generation or two behind, meaning he is squishier and hits softer than the new kids on the block. He absolutely requires Limit Breaking (and likely Emblems) to survive in today's environment.
Special Skill: Claws of the Hunter
"Claws of the Hunter" is a mixed bag. At
, hitting three targets is standard, but the damage distribution is a bit underwhelming.
280% to the target is decent, but "minor damage" to nearby enemies is often negligible against modern defenses. It softens them up, but rarely secures a kill on the wings.

This is his main selling point. The Bone Trap Fiend absorbs healing (35% of max health) and deals increasing damage (starting at 45% attack). The ramping damage (up to 93%) sounds scary, but in practice, fiends often get healed away before they reach full potency. However, in modes where healing is restricted, these little biters become permanent pests.
Average (10 tiles). With a Lv. 23 Mana Troop, you can drop this to 9 tiles, which is a crucial breakpoint for 4-star tournaments.
Target: ~656 Attack * 280% = ~1836 raw damage value (before defense).
Nearby: Minor damage is typically half, so ~140% = ~918 raw damage value.
These numbers are low compared to modern hitters who might deal 350%+ to three targets.
Healing Absorption: On a 4-star hero with ~1500 HP, the fiend must absorb ~525 HP. That's a significant chunk of a healer's output (often negating a full heal from an average healer).
DoT: The damage starts low but if ignored for 5 turns, it adds up significantly.
adds a flat 150 damage over 3 turns (50 per turn). It's not huge, but it's guaranteed, free damage that bypasses defense.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Plains Hunter family brings some very modern mechanics to an older stat line.

The bonus provides Attack/Defense/Health boosts when teamed up with other Plains Hunters. While nice, you are unlikely to run a full team of them in 4-star content, making this less impactful than it appears on paper.
As mentioned, this applies a DoT to a random enemy upon casting. It refreshes and stacks damage if reapplied. It’s a nice little "bonus tap" that can finish off low-HP enemies.
to drop received Special Skill damage to 1. It's pure RNG, but when it procs, it can win you the match. Don't rely on it, but celebrate it when it happens.

is... situational. It fits his theme (he uses fiends, so he knows how to fight them), but generally, you'd prefer an offensive power like Attack Up or a defensive one like Damage Reduction. It helps him in the "Fiend vs Fiend" mirror matches, but otherwise offers zero value.
Upon release, Maheegan was a solid option for players lacking the premium event 4-stars. His fiends were a relatively rare mechanic at the 4-star level.
The stat gap is his biggest enemy. Modern 4-stars simply do everything he does, but better and faster. Heroes like
(Construct family) hit harder and have wilder mechanics.
. He is no longer a general-purpose hero. You wouldn't take him into a standard war attack against a modern team. However, he retains value in
tournaments and potentially Rush Attack modes where his average speed is mitigated, and his fiends can overwhelm teams relying on healers.
As of this review, Maheegan does not have a costume.

Maximizes the initial hit and the fiend damage (which scales off attack).
He becomes extremely fragile and might die to a few slashes from modern snipers.
path is safer. His primary utility is the Fiend (health absorption) and the Hunter's Mark, neither of which strictly require massive tile damage to be effective.
Summoning fiends at the 4-star level is excellent for controlling healers.
The 10% damage reduction can be a lifesaver.
Monk is a top-tier class for resisting key ailments like silence or mana block.
Hunter's Mark adds chip damage that helps wear down enemies.
He is statistically weak compared to 2024/2025 heroes.
In a world of Fast/Very Fast threats, Average feels slow without a high-impact payoff.

This is his playground. Since enemies cannot heal, the only way to remove his Fiends is by overwriting them with minions. If they don't bring minion summoners, your Fiends deal permanent damage for the entire match.
His fiends don't count as buffs, but his passives help him survive.
Useful for depth when you need to stop enemy healing.
Maheegan works best when you lean into his attrition style.
Maheegan - C. Gormek - Waddles - Azmia - Maheegan
C. Gormek destroys minions (which counters the counter to Maheegan), while Maheegan floods them with fiends. Waddles controls mana.
Boldtusk boosts Maheegan's attack (making fiends hit harder). Cillian and Maheegan provide a mix of direct damage and revival denial.
Minions will push fiends off the hero card if the slots are full.
Specific fiend counters (though rare at 4-star play).
Turns fiends into minions (a hard counter).
You can only have 3 fiends per enemy. If you bring too many summoners, you overwrite your own fresh fiends.

If you pulled Maheegan early, he is a great asset. His fiends will confuse and wreck low-level teams that rely on a single healer. Level him, but maybe hold off on Limit Breaking him until you are sure you enjoy his playstyle.

. If you take that tournament mode seriously, he is worth keeping on the bench. Otherwise, he is likely a pass. Do not spend Alpha Aethers on him unless you have an abundance of resources and a deep love for wolves.

"Combined with a mana troop, his friends are pretty vicious for a nine-tile hitter... he should be a good one for raid tournaments."
"Typical my luck… I just got a second one of this pile of garbage... I want the 3-star heroes! Those are amazing."

Maheegan is the classic case of a hero with a cool kit trapped in an aging body. He tries to do a bit of everything—hit, summon, dot, passive resist—but doesn't excel enough in any single area to be a superstar today. However, in the specific lawless wasteland of a
, this wolf is an apex predator.
Keep one for tournaments, feed the dupes.
